techniques.evidence_quote: The emails linked to what we assess was a credential theft site hosted at irss[.]securesusa[.]com.
The domains were a mix of attacker-owned domains... The ones owned by the attackers were: managed7.com, street7news.org, street7market.net, docfilessa.com (ATT&CK: T1583.001).

legitimate domains with weak DMARC protections (which make it easier for attackers to spoof or send on behalf of the domain without being blocked by common email security controls) (ATT&CK: T1584.001).
The attack began with a set of compromised access keys... Once they obtained the AWS access key, the attacker's first move was a simple GetCallerIdentity request (ATT&CK: T1078.004).

Within a span of just ten seconds, we observed a burst of PutAccountDetails requests that fanned out across all AWS regions — a clear attempt to push the SES account into production mode (ATT&CK: T1098).
They then attempted to escalate their privileges by creating an IAM policy named ses-support-policy and attaching it to the compromised IAM user (ATT&CK: T1098.003).
They issued GetSendQuota and GetAccount calls, both intended to reveal the current state of the SES configuration and whether the account was still restricted to sandbox limits (ATT&CK: T1526).
SES lets adversaries launch phishing at scale from trusted domains, shifting both financial costs and reputational damage onto the victim.

attack_chain.description: Attacker obtained stolen AWS IAM access keys (likely from accidental public exposure in a code repository or developer workstation) and used them to authenticate to the victim's AWS environment.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The attack began with a set of compromised access keys. While the exact vector remains unknown, such keys are often obtained through accidental public exposure (code repositories, misconfigured assets) or theft from a developer workstation.

attack_chain.description: Attacker called GetCallerIdentity to enumerate the identity associated with the compromised key, noting 'ses-' in the key name indicating SES permissions. Then called GetSendQuota and GetAccount to probe the SES configuration and sandbox status.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The attacker's first move was a simple GetCallerIdentity request... The API call response revealed that the access key had 'ses-' embedded in its name... They issued GetSendQuota and GetAccount calls, both intended to reveal the current state of the SES configuration.

attack_chain.description: Within ten seconds, the attacker issued a burst of PutAccountDetails API requests across all AWS regions to move the SES account from sandbox (restricted) mode to production mode, enabling sending to arbitrary recipients at up to 50,000 emails/day. A fraudulent business justification referencing an unrelated construction company website was included to pass AWS review.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Within a span of just ten seconds, we observed a burst of PutAccountDetails requests that fanned out across all AWS regions — a strong indicator of automation and a clear attempt to push the SES account into production mode.

attack_chain.technique_name: Account Manipulation — Support Ticket for Quota Increase
attack_chain.description: Attacker attempted to programmatically open an AWS support ticket via the CreateCase API to further raise SES sending limits beyond the default 50,000/day quota. This failed due to insufficient permissions on the compromised key.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: They tried to open a support ticket programmatically through the CreateCase API, asking AWS to further raise their limits (ATT&CK: T1098), an attempt that failed due to insufficient permissions.

attack_chain.description: After the support ticket attempt failed, attacker tried to escalate privileges by creating a new IAM policy named 'ses-support-policy' and attaching it to the compromised IAM user. This also failed due to insufficient permissions.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: They then attempted to escalate their privileges by creating an IAM policy named ses-support-policy and attaching it to the compromised IAM user (ATT&CK: T1098.003). That effort also failed due to insufficient permissions.

attack_chain.description: Attacker registered and controlled attacker-owned domains (managed7.com, street7news.org, street7market.net, docfilessa.com) and verified them as SES sender identities via the CreateEmailIdentity API. They also added legitimate domains with weak DMARC protections as verified identities.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The attacker started by adding multiple domains as verified identities using the CreateEmailIdentity API. The domains were a mix of attacker-owned domains and legitimate domains with weak DMARC protections.

attack_chain.description: Attacker added legitimate third-party domains with weak DMARC protections as verified SES sender identities, enabling spoofed or on-behalf-of sending that bypasses common email security controls.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: legitimate domains with weak DMARC protections (which make it easier for attackers to spoof or send on behalf of the domain without being blocked by common email security controls).

attack_chain.description: Attacker launched a phishing campaign via SES using the verified sender identities (admin@, billing@, sales@, noreply@ prefixes). Emails impersonated tax form notifications, linking victims to a credential theft site at irss[.]securesusa[.]com hidden behind a redirect from a commercial traffic analysis service to evade detection.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The phishing messages referenced 2024 tax forms... The emails linked to what we assess was a credential theft site hosted at irss[.]securesusa[.]com. To evade detection, the attackers concealed the site behind a redirect provided by a commercial traffic analysis service.

impact_type: SES Abuse for Phishing / Credential Theft
initial_access_vector: Stolen/compromised AWS IAM access keys, likely obtained through accidental public exposure in code repositories or theft from a developer workstation
persistence_mechanism: SES account moved to production mode via PutAccountDetails; attacker-controlled and weak-DMARC domains registered as verified SES sender identities, enabling durable phishing infrastructure within the victim's AWS account
privilege_escalation: Attempted (failed): attacker tried to create IAM policy 'ses-support-policy' and attach it to the compromised IAM user (T1098.003), and to open a support ticket via CreateCase API to raise SES quotas — both blocked by insufficient permissions
